text: The surgeon makes a 5mm subumbilical incision using a #15 scalpel blade. A fan-shaped infiltration of the subcutaneous fat is performed with a saline, lidocaine, and adrenaline mixture using a 50ml syringe and a 20-gauge cannula. After a 10-minute wait, liposuction is conducted with a dedicated syringe and circulation procurement system. The peri-umbilical incision is then closed, and the lipoaspirate is transferred to syringes for separation of excess fluids from the fat tissue.
The surgeon processes mesenchymal cells using an adipose tissue device, preparing the cells for deployment in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a fistuloscope to visualize the anatomy of the fistula tract, identifying residual abscesses, side tracts, and the internal opening. The tract is curetted and cleaned after infiltration with adrenalized saline. An advancement flap is created using mucosa, submucosa, and muscular fibers to cover the internal opening, with the distal margin secured by absorbable sutures. Lipoaspirate is infiltrated around the external opening and along the entire fistula tract.
